past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 15 years ago, variably controlled
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 10 years ago
Obstructive sleep apnea, non-compliant with CPAP
Chronic kidney disease stage 3a (baseline creatinine 1.4 mg/dL)
No known history of atrial fibrillation or cardiac arrhythmia
family_history: Mother had a stroke at age 71
Father died of congestive heart failure at age 74
Sister with hypertension and type 2 diabetes
No family history of bleeding disorders or early-onset dementia

social_history.smoking: Active smoker, 25 pack-year history, currently 1/2 pack per day
social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in an apartment, divorced 8 years ago
social_history.exercise: Minimal, reports walking to and from his truck

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person and place, uncertain about the date (states it is two days earlier than actual). Mildly inattentive, makes errors on serial 7s. Speech is mildly dysarthric with occasional word-finding pauses but able to name objects, repeat phrases, and follow two-step commands. Reading intact. Writing shows mild right-hand clumsiness but is legible.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, briskly reactive. Visual fields full to confrontation but extinguishes on the left with bilateral simultaneous stimulation. No gaze preference at rest, but mild difficulty with sustained leftward gaze. Right lower facial droop, subtle, noticeable with voluntary smile. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Right upper extremity: 5/5 throughout. Right lower extremity: 5/5 throughout. Left upper extremity: 4/5 grip, 4-/5 deltoid, 4/5 wrist extension, mild pronator drift present on left. Left lower extremity: 4+/5 hip flexion, 5-/5 knee extension, 4+/5 ankle dorsiflexion. No fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.sensory: Decreased light touch and pinprick sensation on the left arm and hand. Proprioception intact bilaterally at great toes. Left visual-tactile extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Right upper extremity 2+, right lower extremity 2+. Left upper extremity 2+, left lower extremity 3+ with equivocal plantar response on the left (neither clearly upgoing nor downgoing). Right plantar response is flexor.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose intact bilaterally but slightly clumsy on the left. Heel-to-shin mildly impaired on the left, possibly due to weakness. No truncal ataxia.
neurological_exam.gait: Mildly wide-based, left leg circumduction noted. Able to walk independently but unsteady on tandem gait. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: NIHSS score: 6. Subtle left-sided neglect on line bisection (deviates 1.5 cm to the right). No meningismus. No visual field cut on formal confrontation testing.

patient.chief_complaint: Woke up with slurred speech and left arm clumsiness
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Clarence Davis is a 67-year-old right-handed African American man with a history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidemia, obstructive sleep apnea, and chronic kidney disease stage 3a who presents to the emergency department after waking up at approximately 06:30 this morning with slurred speech and difficulty using his left hand. He reports going to bed at approximately 23:00 last night feeling entirely normal. He woke once around 03:00 to use the bathroom and states he felt fine at that time, though he acknowledges he was groggy and did not perform any fine motor tasks. When he woke at 06:30 for work, he noticed his speech sounded thick and his left hand was clumsy when he tried to button his shirt. He initially attributed this to sleeping on his arm but became concerned when the symptoms persisted after thirty minutes. He drove himself to a nearby urgent care center, which immediately redirected him to our emergency department via ambulance. EMS noted mild dysarthria, subtle left facial droop, and mild left upper extremity weakness on initial evaluation and activated a stroke alert. Fingerstick glucose in the field was 142 mg/dL. The patient arrived in the ED at 08:15. He denies any headache, visual changes, nausea, vomiting, seizure activity, or loss of consciousness. He reports no recent illness, fever, chest pain, or palpitations. He has been compliant with his medications but admits his blood pressure is often elevated when checked at the pharmacy. He also acknowledges that he has not been using his CPAP machine for the past several months because it makes him uncomfortable. His last known well time is conservatively estimated at 23:00 the prior evening, approximately 9 hours and 15 minutes before ED arrival, placing him outside the standard IV alteplase window. However, his self-reported bathroom visit at 03:00 raises the possibility that symptoms began after that time, though this cannot be confirmed.

findings.location: Right corona radiata and right centrum semiovale, with extension into the right posterior frontal subcortical white matter
findings.size: Approximately 2.1 x 1.4 cm in maximal axial dimensions

signal_characteristics.DWI: Hyperintense signal consistent with restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.ADC: Corresponding hypointensity confirming true restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Corresponding hyperintensity suggesting onset at least 4-6 hours prior
signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense signal in the affected region

findings.borders: Relatively well-defined, subcortical location not conforming to a single large vascular territory

findings.location: Bilateral periventricular and subcortical white matter

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Confluent periventricular and scattered subcortical white matter hyperintensities (Fazekas grade 3)
signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion in chronic lesions

additional_observations: Extensive chronic white matter disease complicates assessment of acute changes
Mild generalized cerebral volume loss, somewhat advanced for age

MRA of the circle of Willis: No large vessel occlusion. Mild irregularity of the right MCA M1 segment suggestive of atherosclerotic disease. Bilateral A1 segments patent. Posterior circulation patent.
Several old lacunar infarcts in the bilateral basal ganglia and left thalamus
mri.impression: 1. Acute infarction in the right corona radiata and centrum semiovale with restricted diffusion and corresponding FLAIR hyperintensity, consistent with a subacute ischemic event (estimated 4-6+ hours old). 2. No large vessel occlusion on MRA; mild right MCA atherosclerosis noted. 3. Extensive chronic small vessel ischemic disease (Fazekas 3) with multiple old lacunar infarcts, significantly complicating interpretation. 4. No hemorrhagic transformation.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Acute ischemic infarction, right corona radiata, likely small vessel or branch occlusion
differential_by_imaging.key_features: DWI restriction with ADC confirmation in subcortical location without large vessel occlusion
Patient age and vascular risk factors favor ischemic etiology; no ring enhancement or typical MS pattern
No evidence of hemorrhage on any sequence

recommended_actions: Correlate with clinical presentation for wake-up stroke management
Consider DWI-FLAIR mismatch analysis for potential thrombolysis eligibility
Follow-up imaging in 24-48 hours to assess evolution

findings: Left atrial enlargement by P-wave morphology in V1 (biphasic P wave with deep terminal negative component)
Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria (SV1 + RV5 = 38 mm)
Nonspecific ST-T wave changes in lateral leads
No acute ST elevation or depression
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm at 78 bpm with left atrial enlargement and left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria. Nonspecific lateral ST-T wave changes likely related to LVH. No arrhythmia or acute ischemic changes.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Left atrial enlargement may suggest underlying substrate for paroxysmal atrial fibrillation even though the current rhythm is sinus. Consider prolonged cardiac monitoring to evaluate for occult paroxysmal AF as a potential stroke etiology.

CBC.clinical_significance: Mildly low, may be related to chronic kidney disease

BMP.clinical_significance: Elevated, consistent with known CKD stage 3a
Elevated, consistent with known CKD stage 3a (baseline 1.4 mg/dL)
Elevated, consistent with known diabetes and possible stress hyperglycemia

Lipid Panel.clinical_significance: Above target for patient with vascular disease and multiple risk factors
Low HDL, additional cardiovascular risk factor
Significantly elevated, associated with metabolic syndrome and insulin resistance

Cardiac and Other.clinical_significance: Poorly controlled diabetes, significant stroke risk factor
Mildly elevated, may suggest subclinical cardiac dysfunction or volume overload in CKD setting

labs.interpretation: Laboratory results show mildly elevated creatinine and BUN consistent with the patient's known CKD stage 3a. Glucose is elevated consistent with known diabetes; HbA1c of 8.2% indicates poor glycemic control. Lipid panel shows persistent dyslipidemia despite statin therapy with elevated LDL, low HDL, and significantly elevated triglycerides. Mildly low hemoglobin may reflect anemia of chronic kidney disease. BNP is mildly elevated, warranting cardiac evaluation. Coagulation studies are normal. Troponin is within normal limits, arguing against acute cardiac ischemia.

signal_characteristics.CTA: Approximately 40-50% stenosis of the right MCA M1 segment with calcified plaque. No occlusion. Distal branches patent.

findings.location: Bilateral carotid bifurcations and proximal internal carotid arteries

signal_characteristics.CTA: Right ICA: approximately 30% stenosis with mixed plaque. Left ICA: approximately 40% stenosis with predominantly calcified plaque.

additional_observations: No large vessel occlusion in the anterior or posterior circulation
Vertebrobasilar system is patent without significant stenosis
Aortic arch is type II with mild atheroma
No evidence of dissection in cervical or intracranial vessels
output.impression: 1. Moderate atherosclerotic stenosis (40-50%) of the right MCA M1 segment, likely the symptomatic vessel given the right corona radiata infarct. 2. Mild-to-moderate bilateral carotid bifurcation atherosclerosis without hemodynamically significant stenosis. 3. No large vessel occlusion. 4. No dissection.
recommended_actions: Atherosclerotic intracranial stenosis likely the mechanism; consider dual antiplatelet therapy per SAMMPRIS protocol
Consider further evaluation for embolic sources including prolonged cardiac monitoring

Transthoracic Echocardiogram.clinical_significance: Mildly dilated left atrium, raising suspicion for paroxysmal atrial fibrillation
Consistent with longstanding, poorly controlled hypertension
Elevated filling pressures, consistent with hypertensive heart disease

Transthoracic Echocardiogram.value: No PFO or ASD detected by color Doppler. Bubble study not performed.

output.interpretation: Echocardiogram reveals low-normal left ventricular systolic function with concentric left ventricular hypertrophy and grade II diastolic dysfunction, consistent with longstanding hypertensive heart disease. Left atrium is mildly dilated, which may predispose to atrial fibrillation. No intracardiac thrombus or PFO identified on TTE. Mild aortic sclerosis noted. These findings support the need for prolonged cardiac monitoring to rule out paroxysmal atrial fibrillation as a contributory stroke mechanism.

findings: Predominantly normal sinus rhythm throughout the 72-hour recording
Three short runs of atrial fibrillation detected: longest episode 45 seconds at hour 38, two additional episodes of 12 and 18 seconds
Frequent premature atrial complexes (PACs), 847 in 72 hours (approximately 0.5% burden)
Rare premature ventricular complexes (28 in 72 hours)
output.interpretation: Seventy-two hour Holter monitoring reveals predominantly normal sinus rhythm with three brief paroxysms of atrial fibrillation (longest 45 seconds). Frequent premature atrial complexes are noted, suggesting an irritable atrial substrate. No significant pauses or ventricular arrhythmias.
output.clinical_correlation: The detection of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, even brief episodes, in the setting of cryptogenic-appearing stroke with left atrial enlargement, strongly supports a cardioembolic mechanism. Current guidelines support anticoagulation for secondary stroke prevention in this setting (CHA2DS2-VASc score >= 2 with prior stroke). Consider transitioning from antiplatelet to anticoagulation therapy.

Hypercoagulability Panel.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, may contribute to vascular risk; can be exacerbated by CKD. Consider folate and B12 supplementation.

output.interpretation: Hypercoagulability panel is largely unremarkable with no hereditary or acquired thrombophilia identified. Homocysteine is mildly elevated at 16.8 umol/L, which may be related to chronic kidney disease and could contribute modestly to vascular risk. No antiphospholipid antibodies detected. The stroke etiology is most likely atherosclerotic or cardioembolic rather than thrombophilic.

findings.location: Right internal carotid artery at the bifurcation
Left internal carotid artery at the bifurcation

additional_observations: Bilateral common carotid arteries show diffuse intimal thickening consistent with generalized atherosclerosis
Bilateral vertebral arteries are patent with antegrade flow
output.impression: 1. Mild bilateral carotid bifurcation atherosclerosis, both less than 50% stenosis by NASCET criteria. 2. Patent bilateral vertebral arteries. 3. Diffuse atherosclerotic disease consistent with multiple vascular risk factors. The extracranial carotid disease is not hemodynamically significant and unlikely to be the primary stroke mechanism.
recommended_actions: Aggressive risk factor modification including smoking cessation, blood pressure control, and lipid optimization
Follow-up carotid duplex in 12 months to monitor for progression
Focus stroke workup on intracranial atherosclerosis and cardiac monitoring

output.query: wake-up stroke DWI-FLAIR mismatch thrombolysis guidelines

results.title: MRI-Guided Thrombolysis for Stroke with Unknown Time of Onset (WAKE-UP Trial)
results.authors: Thomalla G, Simonsen CZ, et al.
results.key_finding: IV alteplase improved functional outcome in wake-up stroke patients selected by DWI-FLAIR mismatch (DWI positive, FLAIR negative), suggesting recent onset (<4.5h).

results.title: Extending the Time for Thrombolysis in Emergency Neurological Deficits (EXTEND Trial)
results.authors: Ma H, Campbell BCV, et al.
results.key_finding: IV alteplase was beneficial in patients 4.5-9 hours from onset or wake-up stroke, selected by perfusion imaging showing salvageable tissue.

results.title: 2019 AHA/ASA Guideline Update: Wake-up Stroke Management
results.authors: Powers WJ, Rabinstein AA, et al.
results.key_finding: In wake-up stroke patients with DWI-FLAIR mismatch on MRI, IV alteplase can be considered (Class IIa, Level B-R).

output.summary: The WAKE-UP trial established that DWI-FLAIR mismatch can identify wake-up stroke patients who may benefit from IV thrombolysis. Patients with DWI-positive but FLAIR-negative lesions (suggesting onset within approximately 4.5 hours) showed improved functional outcomes with alteplase. However, in this patient, the presence of FLAIR hyperintensity corresponding to the DWI lesion suggests the infarct is beyond the 4.5-hour window, making the patient ineligible for thrombolysis based on WAKE-UP criteria. The EXTEND trial offers an alternative pathway using perfusion imaging to select patients up to 9 hours from onset.

interactions: Aspirin 81 mg: Increased bleeding risk when combined with anticoagulation. Discontinue aspirin when initiating anticoagulation for AF-related stroke prevention, unless dual therapy specifically indicated (e.g., recent coronary stent).
contraindications: CKD stage 3a (CrCl estimated 45-59 mL/min): Apixaban does not require dose adjustment at this level of renal function. Standard dose 5 mg BID is appropriate. Dose reduction to 2.5 mg BID only if 2 of 3 criteria met: age >= 80, weight <= 60 kg, creatinine >= 1.5 mg/dL.
warnings: Patient's creatinine is 1.5 mg/dL (meets 1 of 3 dose-reduction criteria); standard dose is appropriate unless age >= 80 or weight <= 60 kg
Monitor renal function periodically as CKD may progress
Ensure concurrent metformin dose remains appropriate for renal function
output.formulary_status: Preferred formulary agent for non-valvular AF
alternatives: Rivaroxaban 20 mg daily (requires dose reduction to 15 mg daily if CrCl 15-50 mL/min)
Edoxaban 60 mg daily (reduce to 30 mg if CrCl 15-50 mL/min or weight <= 60 kg)
Warfarin with INR target 2.0-3.0 (requires monitoring, but no renal dose adjustment)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke, right corona radiata, likely multifactorial etiology (intracranial atherosclerosis with occult paroxysmal atrial fibrillation)

differential.key_distinguishing: Subcortical location and size are consistent with lacunar mechanism, but the lesion is slightly larger than typical lacune and coronary radiata infarcts can also result from branch atheromatous disease or embolism

differential.diagnosis: Cardioembolic stroke from paroxysmal atrial fibrillation
differential.key_distinguishing: Left atrial enlargement on ECG and echo, brief AF on prolonged monitoring; however, single subcortical lesion is atypical for cardioembolism which usually produces cortical or large wedge-shaped infarcts

differential.diagnosis: Intracranial atherosclerotic disease with branch occlusion
differential.key_distinguishing: Right MCA M1 atherosclerosis on CTA with corona radiata infarct in the right MCA perforator territory; consistent with branch atheromatous disease
No hemorrhage on MRI; DWI restriction in vascular territory confirms ischemic etiology
Acute onset, DWI restriction pattern, patient age and risk factor profile strongly favor ischemic stroke; no enhancing mass or ring enhancement

optimal_actions.action: Activate stroke alert, perform rapid assessment including NIHSS, establish last known well time, and obtain fingerstick glucose
optimal_actions.expected_finding: NIHSS 6, LKW conservatively 23:00 (~9 hours prior), glucose 142 mg/dL

optimal_actions.action: Obtain emergent non-contrast CT head to exclude hemorrhage
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No acute hemorrhage; chronic white matter changes

optimal_actions.action: Obtain stat labs including CBC, BMP, coagulation panel, troponin, HbA1c, and lipid panel
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal coagulation, elevated glucose, elevated HbA1c, dyslipidemia, elevated creatinine at baseline

optimal_actions.action: Obtain 12-lead ECG to evaluate for arrhythmia
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal sinus rhythm with left atrial enlargement and LVH; no AF on initial ECG

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI with DWI and FLAIR to assess for DWI-FLAIR mismatch and determine thrombolysis eligibility in this wake-up stroke
optimal_actions.expected_finding: DWI positive with corresponding FLAIR hyperintensity, suggesting infarct is beyond 4.5-hour window; patient ineligible for thrombolysis by WAKE-UP criteria

optimal_actions.action: Obtain CTA head and neck to evaluate for large vessel occlusion and vascular anatomy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No LVO; moderate right MCA M1 atherosclerosis likely the symptomatic lesion

optimal_actions.action: Initiate dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + clopidogrel) for 21 days given minor stroke (NIHSS <= 3-5 guideline, though NIHSS 6 is borderline) or high-dose statin, then transition to antiplatelet monotherapy pending cardiac monitoring results
Order prolonged cardiac monitoring (72-hour Holter or implantable loop recorder) to evaluate for paroxysmal atrial fibrillation given left atrial enlargement and cryptogenic-appearing stroke
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Brief paroxysms of AF detected on 72-hour monitoring

optimal_actions.action: Order transthoracic echocardiogram to evaluate for structural heart disease and embolic source
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Concentric LVH, diastolic dysfunction, mildly dilated left atrium

optimal_actions.action: Once paroxysmal AF confirmed on Holter, initiate anticoagulation with apixaban for secondary stroke prevention and discontinue antiplatelet therapy
Admit to stroke unit for monitoring, dysphagia screening, DVT prophylaxis, and early rehabilitation assessment
Optimize vascular risk factors: intensify antihypertensive regimen, optimize glycemic control, intensify statin therapy, counsel on smoking cessation, and refer for CPAP re-evaluation

critical_actions: Establish last known well time and recognize this as a wake-up stroke outside the standard IV tPA window
Obtain emergent CT to exclude hemorrhage
Obtain brain MRI with DWI and FLAIR to evaluate DWI-FLAIR mismatch for potential thrombolysis eligibility
Recognize left atrial enlargement on ECG as a clue to occult atrial fibrillation and pursue prolonged cardiac monitoring
Order prolonged cardiac monitoring (Holter or loop recorder) given cryptogenic-appearing stroke with left atrial enlargement
Initiate anticoagulation once paroxysmal AF is confirmed, rather than antiplatelet therapy alone
Address multiple modifiable vascular risk factors including smoking, hypertension, diabetes, and dyslipidemia
contraindicated_actions: Administering IV alteplase based solely on symptom assessment without MRI DWI-FLAIR mismatch evaluation in a wake-up stroke with unknown onset time
Accepting the initial normal sinus rhythm ECG as definitive evidence against AF without pursuing prolonged cardiac monitoring
Aggressively lowering blood pressure below 140/90 in the first 24 hours (risk of worsening ischemia in the acute period)
Performing mechanical thrombectomy in the absence of large vessel occlusion
Initiating anticoagulation simultaneously with dual antiplatelet therapy (excessive bleeding risk)
Dismissing the stroke as simply a lacunar infarct without comprehensive etiologic workup
key_reasoning_points: Wake-up stroke presents a diagnostic challenge because the exact onset time is unknown, making standard thrombolysis eligibility uncertain and requiring MRI-based selection (DWI-FLAIR mismatch per WAKE-UP trial)
The presence of FLAIR hyperintensity corresponding to the DWI lesion in this case suggests the infarct is older than 4.5 hours, making the patient ineligible for thrombolysis by WAKE-UP criteria
Left atrial enlargement on the initial ECG, despite sinus rhythm, is an important clue that should prompt investigation for paroxysmal atrial fibrillation
Extensive chronic white matter disease (Fazekas 3) complicates MRI interpretation and may initially obscure the acute lesion or lead to overestimation of acute ischemic burden

The stroke mechanism is initially ambiguous: intracranial atherosclerosis (right MCA stenosis), small vessel disease (lacunar-type location), and cardioembolism (later-discovered paroxysmal AF) are all plausible, requiring systematic workup
Detection of even brief AF episodes in the setting of prior stroke changes management from antiplatelet to anticoagulation therapy, which is a pivotal treatment decision

Multiple poorly controlled vascular risk factors (active smoking, uncontrolled hypertension, poorly controlled diabetes, dyslipidemia, untreated OSA) all contributed to this event and must be addressed for secondary prevention

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate - wake-up stroke with subtle right-sided neglect and mild dysarthria, no prior AF known but left atrial enlargement on ECG provides a clue. Vascular risk factors present. Extensive chronic white matter disease complicates MRI interpretation.
clinical_pearls: Wake-up strokes account for approximately 20-25% of all ischemic strokes and require MRI-based selection for thrombolysis eligibility
Left atrial enlargement on ECG in a stroke patient without known AF should always prompt prolonged cardiac monitoring
Approximately 20-30% of initially cryptogenic strokes are found to have paroxysmal AF on prolonged monitoring
The distinction between antiplatelet and anticoagulation therapy for secondary prevention is one of the most consequential decisions in stroke management
